summary: Plan 05 is executable after repair: readiness now has deterministic eight-check output, real wall-clock budgets, cancellation-safe single-flight caching, bounded per-label worker admission, isolated and timed connection-bound PostgreSQL probes, safe same-descriptor filesystem probes, durable orphan reconciliation, and mechanical ALB plus zero-skip PostgreSQL acceptance gates.

review_basis.head: 33221d28e2ad25cd14f39c8a416e1cd189bc4d06
review_basis.baseline: Current create_app/lifespan, SessionService orphan cleanup, execution-to-Landscape ordering, session engine behavior, FilesystemPayloadStore, repaired Plans 02-04, and downstream Plans 10/12.

warnings.detail: Python cannot forcibly stop a filesystem syscall already running in a worker. The plan bounds this to one unresolved future per closed label, drains late outcomes, returns static saturation failures, and documents operator remediation.

resolved_findings.resolution: Filesystem readiness now mirrors the doctor contract: no mkdir, lstat and unsafe-mode rejection, exclusive mkstemp creation, immediate unlink, same-descriptor write/fsync/read, and deterministic cleanup failure.
PostgreSQL readiness uses raw AWS URLs, isolated one-shot engines, bounded pool/connect/statement timeouts, and SELECT plus schema probe on the same Connection.
OIDC and Entra auth checks now require client_id as well as their issuer or tenant and audience fields.
Per-label and route deadlines use loop wall time; the five-second route timeout includes cache selection and follower waiting.
A five-worker, five-label registry admits at most one unresolved future per label and refuses duplicate submissions, preventing unbounded ThreadPoolExecutor queue growth.
Future callbacks and close-time cancellation occur outside the registry lock, with immediate-completion and concurrent-close deadlock regressions.
Both concurrent and asyncio wrapper futures are cancelled or drained on abandonment, including a loop-exception-handler test proving late secret-bearing failures cannot leak.
The shared cache owns a shielded asyncio Task, so cancellation of the leading request cannot cancel computation or create a second batch.
Runner/cache state is created for both default and AWS modes, while AWS construction remains ordered after Plan 04's pre-bind gate.
Default sqlite memory engines are never checked out from a readiness worker; they return a static paired not-ready remedy, while file-backed SQLite retains live-engine coverage.
PostgreSQL failure injection uses NOLOGIN plus backend termination and an independent fresh-connection failure assertion rather than ineffective role-only CONNECT revocation.
Orphan reconciliation uses exact durable pending, complete, and absent markers with startup and periodic idempotent retry across Landscape and session-marker failure points.

Null Landscape anchors close as normal no-ops; missing non-null anchors receive a distinct durable absent audit-exception outcome, static ERROR signal, operator investigation, and regression coverage.

Plans 00, 10, and 12 now agree on Plan 04 dependency ordering, exact enabled /api/ready ALB health checks with matcher 200 and timeout at least six seconds, and the four-file zero-skip PostgreSQL gate.
The route example uses a lint-clean nested async compute function, and the plan ends with focused, subsystem, Docker, Ruff, format, strict mypy, diff, and Wardline gates.

verification: current create_app, lifespan, SessionService, execution ordering, and SQLite engine behavior reconciled against the plan
exact eight-check order, two-second grouped budgets, five-second route ceiling, and two-second cache contract reviewed
Plan 02 connection-bound probe and Plan 03 filesystem security contracts reconciled
Plan 10 target-group and Plan 12 four-file PostgreSQL handoffs mechanically pinned
three independent post-repair specialist reviews reached GO with no remaining blockers

review_history.summary: Initial review found unsafe pathname probes, second-checkout DB probes, incomplete auth fields, synthetic timing, unbounded executor accumulation, cancellation-owned cache work, event-loop path I/O, incomplete lifespan degradation, ineffective PostgreSQL fault injection, and missing downstream acceptance wiring.
